content:
I have a proof-of-concept of a different register! API as disccused in the thread here: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260128-register-v4-0-aee3a33d9649@nvidia.com/T/#mbdf1acf98fc691df50845553cea46d7c53725ef6.

The code is here https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/commit/d5264f51a48340fc108a75b4c470aab44c298caa (ignore the code quality as it's a POC; I created a helper type per register as it's easy, but in practice I think we don't need one per register, just 4 helper types would be sufficient).

The usage of this API would be:

io.read_reg(REGISTER_NAME) // current: REGISTER_NAME::read(io)
io.read_reg(REGISTER_NAME(base)) // current: REGISTER_NAME::read(io, base)
io.read_reg(REGISTER_ARRAY(index)) // current: REGISTER_ARRAY::read(io, index)
io.read_reg(REGISTER_ARRAY(base, index)) // current: REGISTER_ARRAY::read(io, base, index)

the benefit is that it does not have the Deref issue, and IMO this is more consistent with plain of IO access.

@Danilo Krummrich @Alexandre Courbot
Ok, I see you solved it with an indirection.
I.e. the read_reg() calls back into the register type to execute the actual I/O operation.
Which is the answer to my question "How do we figure out the size, etc."
I think there's also some venue in making it slightly more generic and perhaps just use io.read(REGISTER_REFERENCE).
To me this looks nicer than I anticipated, especially that now the code isn't that different from a C code reading register with a #define-ed offset, but now we have type and bounds checking
I think it's a good solution, but I don't think we gain much from it, do we? I.e. having the custom trait instead of Deref isn't more complicated.
I think this is more natural to read. It also does not require any additional AsIo trait impl. You can just use the register.
With this properly cleaned up and implemented i think this will also be less LOC and easier to understand than the current macro
(You can think the RegisterRef in my POC is just a description of a register offset + type)
All actual read/write/update code would be fully generic and there's no need to codegen it for each register anymore.

All actual read/write/update code would be fully generic and there's no need to codegen it for each register anymore.

Yes, I do like this part.

With this properly cleaned up and implemented i think this will also be less LOC and easier to understand than the current macro

(You can think the RegisterRef in my POC is just a description of a register offset + type)

I don't think I can agree with this, but I also won't object going forward with this approach, as it seems reasonable in general. But I'd like to get rid of this icky "_reg" suffix. :slight_smile:
Another reason I like this is that it rhymes with the field projection / field representating type work that @Benno Lossin and I work on. :)
